title: Space Tech Briefing — July 17, 2026
summary:
Good morning, Space Explorers! And a very warm welcome to your daily dose of the cosmos. It’s Friday, July 17, 2026, and we're beaming live with the latest and greatest from the final frontier! Get ready for a blast-off of news that will ignite your morning.

First up, let’s turn our gaze to our very own star, the Sun! The French space agency CNES has just dropped some fantastic news for your summer reading list. They're suggesting five brilliant books to help us better understand the magnificent star that has captivated humanity for millennia. Whether you're into popular science, graphic novels, thrilling science fiction, or practical observation guides, CNES has curated the perfect poolside reads to deepen your knowledge of our solar powerhouse. Time to grab a book and learn more about that incredible ball of fire!

And speaking of the Sun, mark your calendars, because something truly spectacular is on its way! CNES is buzzing about a major celestial event. On August 12, 2026, just a few weeks from now, the Moon will perfectly align between the Earth and the Sun, gifting us a total solar eclipse! This isn't just any eclipse; it's being hailed as one of the most anticipated total solar eclipses of the entire decade for Europe. Get ready to witness a breathtaking astronomical show!

Now, as we await today's new launches and prepare for that incredible eclipse, let's look back at a major recent development in human spaceflight. We’re talking about Boeing’s Starliner spacecraft and the ongoing challenges within NASA's Commercial Crew program. A new report from NASA’s Office of Inspector General, published on June 30, has once again highlighted the difficulties Starliner is facing. The report points to significant uncertainty regarding when the spacecraft will fly again, and crucially, when it will finally be certified for those vital crew rotation missions to the International Space Station. This continued delay raises serious concerns for NASA, as the agency grapples with the potential risks of relying solely on SpaceX for sending astronauts to the ISS. It's a complex situation, and we'll keep you updated as it unfolds.

That's all for your morning briefing, Space Cadets! What an incredible universe we live in. Stay curious, keep looking up, and have a stellar day! We’ll be back with more tomorrow.
